original_abstract: In contemporary international politics, Gross Domestic Product (GDP) serves as the primary metric for national status and development, yet the pursuit of exponential economic expansion is increasingly linked to catastrophic ecological degradation and social inequality. This paper provides a comparative examination of three influential post-growth frameworks: the steady-state economy, degrowth, and the well-being economy. While these schools of thought differ in their ideological origins and rhetorical strategies—ranging from biophysical equilibrium models to radical cultural critiques—they converge on several transformative policy objectives. These include the replacement of GDP with multidimensional indicators, the implementation of fiscal reforms that tax environmental harms rather than labour, the restructuring of work through hours reduction, and a shift from extractive globalisation toward "bioregional" localisation. By evaluating the evolution and disparate policy impacts of these theories, the article highlights the growing influence of well-being-centered governance in several nations and argues for a fundamental realignment of the international order to prioritise planetary health and human flourishing over material output.
humanized_abstract: GDP remains the global benchmark for measuring state capacity and national progress. This push for ever increasing GDP growth, however, comes at an ever more terrible environmental and socially cost, as world leaders persist in treating sustainable development as a speed bump on a path to ever higher growth. This article compares three of the major post-growth approaches to economics including the steady-state economy, the model of degrowth and the well-being economy approach. While the models vary greatly in their ideological, philosophical or rhetorical frameworks – ranging from a biophysically-driven focus towards equilibrium such as through the principle of renunciation in the case of degrowth or a more critical reinterpretation of culture — they present several policies in common. Most commonly these include alternative measures of progress or GDP beyond the traditional metric that enable measurement of aspects of national progress that are more reflective of societal wealth and human well-being; specific tax reform measures designed to reallocate the burden from work and towards environmentally harmful activities; adjustment of labour markets, including reforms to pricing mechanisms that reflect real cost of the most essential productive resources or reduction of work hours; and a switch from an extractive globalisation to more bioregional forms of commerce, which act to more strongly embed economic networks in their respective territory’s ecological systems in order to conserve nature and be more effectively support people. Understanding the post-growth movement of ideas – including looking at some of the on-the-ground policy impacts and lessons learned from its different formulations – a well-being-focused political economy of post-scalar economics is articulated as a necessary goal for achieving a post-GDP and sustainable development politics at the international level, centred on the renewal of the global economic order through protection of the climate and improved quality of life for all.
